weeks.weekly_summary: Establish aerobic base with easy runs and one tempo session. Building from recent Strava average of 33 km/week, Week 1 targets ~40 km across 5 runs with moderate intensity distribution.
weeks.coaching_notes: Recent 14-day average shows 33 km in 5 runs at easy pace (5:58–6:05/km) with one faster effort. This week establishes the base load and introduces one structured tempo session. HR data (avg 161) confirms aerobic work zone. Keep all easy runs relaxed at 6:05/km or slightly slower; this is foundational work. The tempo run at 5:21/km will be short (~8 km) and structured to build lactate threshold without overloading.
weeks.next_week_preview: Week 2 increases volume to ~44 km (+10%) with a progressive long run building to 14 km. Second quality session (VO2 max repeats) added; maintain 80/20 intensity split.

days.title: Easy 8 km
days.description: Relaxed aerobic run at 6:05/km. Focus on smooth cadence and steady breathing. This is foundational aerobic base work—keep HR in Zone 2 (conversational pace).
days.notes: Block starter—establish consistent running pattern.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Active recovery day. Light stretching or mobility work encouraged.
days.coach_analysis: Good commitment to the plan—49 minutes of gym work with an average HR of 111 bpm suggests you kept the intensity controlled, which is exactly right for a cross-training day. This light-to-moderate effort will build supportive strength without hammering your central nervous system before your next running session. You're balancing the training week well.

days.title: Tempo 6 × 1 km @ 5:21/km (12% faster than easy pace)
days.description: Warm up 1.5 km easy at 6:05/km. Run 6 × 1 km repeats at tempo pace (5:21/km) with 90 seconds recovery jog (6:30/km) between. Cool down 0.5 km easy. This builds lactate threshold and trains you to sustain faster paces.
days.notes: First quality session—keep effort controlled; do not go anaerobic.
days.coach_analysis: Solid execution of your football commitment—you maintained an aerobic-focused effort at 158 bpm average, which is right in that sustainable cross-training zone for building work capacity without compromising your running. The 5.75 km over 68 minutes shows you stayed engaged with good intensity variation typical of football, hitting the aerobic and leg-strength targets you planned for. This session delivered exactly what you needed.

days.title: Long Run 12 km easy @ 6:05/km
days.description: Sustained easy effort at long run pace (6:05/km). Focus on controlled effort and fueling strategy. Drink water every 2–3 km; take simple carbs (sports drink or gels) if running >60 min.
days.key_focus: Long run aerobic base; fueling practice
days.notes: Baseline long run—establishes weekly endurance stimulus.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Active recovery. Light walking, foam rolling, or yoga recommended.

days.title: Easy 7 km
days.description: Conversational pace at 6:05/km. Steady aerobic run to build volume. HR should stay in Zone 2.
days.coach_analysis: You substituted a restorative Pilates session with a hard evening run at 163 bpm average heart rate and 5:58/km pace—this is a significant deviation from the planned low-intensity cross-training. While the run itself looks solid, you've missed the opportunity for active recovery and mobility work that your body likely needs. Going forward, honor your cross-training days as planned, especially after harder running sessions.

coach_adjustment.change: Complete the Pilates session as originally planned before returning to running workouts
coach_adjustment.reason: Substituting structured recovery work with hard running increases injury risk and prevents the adaptations that cross-training provides

days.title: Easy 5 km
days.description: Short easy run at 6:05/km. Light effort to close out the week and prepare for Week 2 build.
days.notes: End-of-week consolidation.
days.coach_analysis: You swapped the planned easy run for a 44-minute pilates session—a smart cross-training choice that supports your running without the impact stress. Your average HR of 96 bpm stayed in the aerobic zone, maintaining fitness while giving your legs active recovery. This fits well into your training week, though make sure tomorrow's run feels fresh and you're not accumulating fatigue from back-to-back harder efforts.

weeks.weekly_summary: Volume increases to ~44 km (+10% from Week 1). Long run grows to 14 km. Two quality sessions introduced: tempo run and VO2 max repeats. Intensity distribution remains 80% easy, 20% hard.
weeks.coaching_notes: Week 2 builds on Week 1 foundation. The long run extends by 2 km to 14 km (progressive tolerance). Second quality session (VO2 max repeats) is shorter in duration to avoid overload; space it 2 days after the tempo run. Easy runs remain at 6:05/km. Monitor cumulative fatigue; if HR drift increases or legs feel heavy, scale back the VO2 repeats to 4 × 1 km.
weeks.next_week_preview: Week 3 increases volume to ~47 km (+5% from Week 2). Tempo run duration extended, long run to 16 km. Two quality sessions maintained with increasing intensity; peak training stimulus approaches.

days.title: Easy 8 km
days.description: Relaxed run at easy pace (6:05/km). Focus on efficient form and breathing. Build aerobic base.
days.coach_analysis: Excellent work swapping your schedule flexibly—that's smart training management. You nailed the pace at 5:59/km (right on the 6:05/km target), but your average HR of 167.8 bpm ran considerably higher than the prescribed 65–75% max HR zone, suggesting you pushed the effort harder than intended despite hitting the right speed. This could indicate you were running into headwind, terrain challenges, or simply having a naturally elevated HR day—monitor this closely on your next easy run to ensure true conversational-pace recovery work.

coach_adjustment.change: Dial back intensity slightly or extend recovery focus; consider a shorter, genuinely conversational easy run
coach_adjustment.reason: HR running 20%+ above target zone suggests aerobic work was harder than planned—prioritize true easy-paced recovery before any harder efforts

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Recovery day. Mobility work or light stretching recommended.

days.title: VO2 Max: 5 × 1 km @ 5:10/km (10 sec faster than tempo)
days.description: Warm up 1.5 km easy at 6:05/km. Run 5 × 1 km repeats at VO2 max pace (5:10/km, ~10 sec faster than tempo) with 2 min recovery jog (6:30/km) between. Cool down 0.5 km easy. This develops VO2 max and neuromuscular power.
days.notes: Shorter repeats than tempo; focus on holding pace consistently.

days.title: Long Run 14 km easy @ 6:05/km
days.description: Extended long run at easy pace (6:05/km). Focus on steady rhythm and fueling. Drink water every 2 km; take carbs every 30–40 min. This builds aerobic capacity and race-specific endurance.
days.notes: Long run grows by 2 km—gauge recovery; sleep well Sunday night.
days.coach_analysis: You ran a moderate-paced 4.2km evening run at 147.7 bpm when a complete rest day was prescribed. While the pace and heart rate suggest controlled effort, this still represents active recovery rather than true rest after football. Consider honoring planned rest days more strictly to allow full CNS and muscular recovery—your body needs these days to adapt to training stress.

coach_adjustment.change: Add 5-10 min easy recovery pace if it's a hard session, or keep the next session unchanged if already marked easy
coach_adjustment.reason: Since you didn't fully rest today, ensure the next running session accounts for this incomplete recovery to avoid cumulative fatigue

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Active recovery. Foam rolling and stretching recommended.
days.coach_analysis: Solid execution of your easy run foundation work. You nailed the pace at 6:05/km (only 6 seconds slower than planned) and covered the full 7.64km distance. However, your average HR of 175.7 bpm is significantly elevated for an easy run—aim to keep these aerobic sessions under 160 bpm to ensure you're truly working at a conversational, sustainable intensity.

coach_adjustment.change: Reduce pace to 6:20-6:30/km to bring HR into 155-165 bpm zone
coach_adjustment.reason: Your HR suggests you're running harder than easy intensity; slower pacing will build aerobic base more efficiently and reduce injury risk

days.title: Tempo 2 × 4 km @ 5:21/km with 3 min recovery
days.description: Warm up 1 km easy at 6:05/km. Run 2 × 4 km repeats at tempo pace (5:21/km) with 3 min recovery jog (6:30/km) between. Cool down 1 km easy. Longer tempo blocks improve sustained lactate threshold.
days.notes: Two quality sessions this week—Tuesday tempo is the second; sufficient recovery between them.

days.title: Easy 5 km
days.description: Short, easy run at 6:05/km. Consolidate the week and prepare for Week 3 buildup.
days.notes: Light effort to close the week.
days.coach_analysis: You nailed the distance and duration, but your average HR of 151.3 bpm is running into Zone 3 territory when Zone 2 easy should sit around 130-140 bpm for true recovery. Your pace of 6:33/km reflects this—you were running with intention rather than genuine ease. Next time, actively slow down and focus on conversational breathing; an easy run should feel almost boring.

weeks.weekly_summary: Volume reaches peak of ~47 km (+5% from Week 2). Long run extends to 16 km. Tempo intensity increases and VO2 max repeats become more challenging. This is the cumulative peak of the block before Week 4 recovery.
weeks.coaching_notes: Week 3 is the physiological peak. Long run reaches 16 km, testing aerobic capacity and fueling discipline. Tempo work becomes 3 × 3 km (more total volume at threshold), and VO2 repeats increase to 6 × 800 m (shorter distance, higher intensity). Monitor fatigue carefully—this is a high-stress week. Sleep 7–9 hours nightly. If fatigue score > 70 or HR drift exceeds 10 bpm on easy runs, scale VO2 session to 5 × 800 m. This block transitions from pure Base Building into Aerobic Development work.
weeks.next_week_preview: Week 4 RECOVERY: Volume drops to ~38 km (−20% from Week 3). Long run reduces to 13 km (20% cut). One quality session maintained (light tempo or easy progression). Focus shifts to consolidation and injury prevention before next block.

days.title: Easy 8 km
days.description: Comfortable aerobic run at 6:05/km. Keep effort conversational. Build volume steadily.
days.coach_analysis: You turned a planned rest day into active recovery with 51 minutes of pilates—a smart choice that kept your HR moderate (119 bpm) without heavy impact stress. This low-intensity cross-training actually supports running recovery by improving core stability and mobility, rather than hindering it. You're well-positioned for the week ahead.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Recovery day. Prepare for Saturday VO2 session with mobility work.

days.title: VO2 Max: 6 × 800 m @ 5:05/km (5:05 min/km pace)
days.description: Warm up 1.5 km easy at 6:05/km. Run 6 × 800 m (0.8 km) repeats at 5:05/km with 90 sec recovery jog (6:30/km) between. Cool down 0.5 km easy. This is a VO2 max stimulus—higher intensity, shorter duration. Develops neuromuscular power and aerobic capacity.
days.notes: Peak quality session this week—high intensity. Ensure full recovery before long run.

days.title: Long Run 16 km easy @ 6:05/km
days.description: Long run at steady easy pace (6:05/km). Fueling is critical at this distance. Drink water every 2 km; consume carbs every 30–40 min (sports drink, gels, or real food). This builds marathon-specific aerobic endurance and tests your fueling strategy.
days.notes: Peak long run of the block—monitor for overtraining signs (persistent elevated HR, heaviness in legs).

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Full recovery day. Serious sleep and nutrition focus.

days.title: Tempo 3 × 3 km @ 5:21/km with 2 min recovery
days.description: Warm up 1 km easy at 6:05/km. Run 3 × 3 km repeats at tempo pace (5:21/km) with 2 min recovery jog (6:30/km) between. Cool down 1 km easy. Longer tempo blocks build sustained lactate threshold—core for marathon success.
days.notes: Second quality session of Week 3—spacing from VO2 session (Saturday) provides recovery.

days.title: Easy 6 km
days.description: Short, easy run at 6:05/km. Consolidate the week and transition into Week 4 recovery.
days.notes: Close of peak week—keep effort light.

weeks.weekly_summary: RECOVERY WEEK: Volume drops to ~38 km (−20% from Week 3). Long run reduces to 13 km. One quality session maintained at lower intensity. Focus shifts to consolidation, injury prevention, and preparing for next training block.
weeks.coaching_notes: Week 4 is the mandatory recovery week. Reduce volume by 20–25% from Week 3 to allow physiological adaptation and CNS recovery. The long run drops from 16 km to 13 km (−20%), and easy runs are slightly shorter. One moderate quality session (light tempo or fartlek) is retained to maintain fitness but at reduced intensity. Easy runs stay at 6:05/km or slightly slower. HR should be lower on easy runs due to freshness. Use this week to address any niggles, optimize sleep (8+ hours), and consolidate the 4-week block adaptations before the next periodization phase.
weeks.next_week_preview: Next 4-week block (Week 5–8) transitions to Aerobic Development phase. Volume returns to ~45–48 km/week with progressive long runs (16–20 km) and increased tempo/threshold work. VO2 max repeats become less frequent; marathon-pace segments introduced in long runs. Build strength and specific endurance toward peak training.

days.title: Easy 7 km
days.description: Relaxed run at 6:05/km. Recovery week—keep HR low and effort minimal. Allow the body to consolidate adaptations.
days.notes: Shorter volume than Weeks 1–3—ease into recovery.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Active recovery. Light yoga or foam rolling recommended.

days.title: Easy Tempo: 2 × 2 km @ 5:30/km with 2 min recovery
days.description: Warm up 1 km easy at 6:05/km. Run 2 × 2 km repeats at a comfortable tempo (5:30/km—slightly slower than threshold, focusing on form over speed) with 2 min recovery jog (6:30/km) between. Cool down 0.5 km easy. This is a maintenance effort, not a push—retain fitness without stressing the system.
days.notes: Recovery-week quality session—moderate intensity only. Focus on smooth running.

days.title: Long Run 13 km easy @ 6:05/km
days.description: Easy long run at 6:05/km. This is 3 km shorter than Week 3 (−20% cut for recovery). Keep it relaxed and conversational. Fueling same as longer runs—water every 2 km, carbs every 30–40 min.
days.notes: Reduced long run for recovery—do not push the pace.

days.title: Rest Day
days.description: Recovery day. Good sleep and nutrition priority.

days.title: Easy 5 km
days.description: Short, easy run at 6:05/km. Minimal stress—just move and recover.

days.title: Easy 5.5 km
days.description: Easy run at 6:05/km to close the 4-week block. Light effort prepares you for Week 5 ramp-up.
days.notes: End-of-block easy run—prepare mentally and physically for Aerobic Development phase ahead.

roadmap.weekly_summary: Transition to Aerobic Development. Volume returns to ~46 km (+20% from Week 4 recovery). Long run grows to 17 km. Tempo work continues at threshold pace; VO2 sessions shift toward longer intervals (1.5–2 km reps) to build sustained aerobic power. Two quality sessions per week.

Volume builds to ~49 km (+5% from Week 5). Long run extends to 19 km—increasing aerobic capacity. Tempo duration increases (4 × 3 km). Threshold repeats become longer (1.5 km reps) to bridge VO2 max and marathon-pace work. Maintain polarized intensity.

key_sessions: Long 20 km with 4 km @ marathon pace embedded
roadmap.weekly_summary: Peak volume of Aerobic Development block: ~51 km. Long run reaches 20 km with first marathon-pace segment (4 km MP inserted in the latter half, teaching pace discipline at fatigue). Tempo work extends to longer continuous blocks. Threshold repeats lengthen to 2 km reps—building sustained threshold capacity essential for marathon success.

key_sessions: Long 18 km with 6 km @ marathon pace
roadmap.weekly_summary: Recovery/transition week bridging Aerobic Development and Peak Training. Volume dips to ~42 km (−18% from Week 7). Long run holds 18 km but increases marathon-pace stimulus (6 km MP). Quality sessions maintain intensity but reduced volume—preparing CNS and aerobic system for Peak Training phase with higher marathon-pace proportion and longer hard segments ahead.

coaching_overview: This 4-week Base Building block establishes the aerobic foundation for a marathon training cycle. Starting from recent Strava data (33 km/week, ~6:00 easy pace), the plan progressively builds to 47 km (peak Week 3) following polarized training principles: 80% easy, 20% hard. Two quality sessions per week (tempo and VO2 max repeats) develop lactate threshold and aerobic power without excess moderate-intensity running. Long runs grow from 12 km (Week 1) to 16 km (Week 3 peak), teaching fueling strategy and race-specific endurance. Week 4 recovery (−20% volume) consolidates adaptations and prevents overtraining. Training paces are anchored in current Strava fitness: easy/long run at 6:05/km (current easy pace), tempo at 5:21/km (~12% faster), and VO2 max at 5:05–5:10/km. The 7-hour weekly time budget is respected throughout. By block end, the athlete is prepared to transition into Aerobic Development (Weeks 5–8) with longer marathon-pace work and peak aerobic stimulus before eventual Peak Training (final 4–8 weeks with high marathon-pace proportion and race rehearsal).

The tempo run at 5:21/km will be short (~8 km) and structured to build lactate threshold without overloading.
